title.en: Your AI Pair: A Brilliant Intern With No Memory

description.en: To work well with a coding AI you need an accurate mental model of what it is: a pattern-matcher that predicts plausible text, not a reasoner that knows things. That single insight explains hallucinated APIs, confident wrong answers, and how to prompt around them.

story_variants.en:
## A Brilliant Intern With No Memory and No Shame

The most useful way to picture your coding AI: an intern who has read **all** the code on the internet, types faster than you can think, is endlessly eager — and has **no memory of yesterday, no idea what's actually in your codebase, and no ability to feel uncertain.**

That last part is the dangerous one. A human junior dev who doesn't know an API will say "hmm, let me check the docs." The AI will *never* do that on its own. It will produce something that **looks** exactly like the right answer, with the same confidence whether it's correct or invented.

### Why It Hallucinates

A large language model doesn't *look up* facts. It predicts the next most plausible token given everything so far. When you ask for `pandas` code, it generates text that *statistically resembles* correct pandas code. Usually that resemblance is so tight the code is correct. But sometimes the most plausible-looking method — `df.drop_duplicates_by('id')` — simply **doesn't exist.** It sounds right. Pandas has `drop_duplicates(subset='id')`. The model pattern-matched the vibe and missed.

This isn't a bug to be fixed. It's how the technology works. Plan around it.

### What This Means In Practice

| Because the AI... | You should... |
|-------------------|---------------|
| has no memory across chats | Re-supply context every session; don't assume it 'remembers' |
| can't see your codebase | Paste the real function signatures and types it must use |
| can't feel uncertain | Treat confident tone as zero evidence; verify anyway |
| predicts plausible text | Be suspicious of any API/method you can't personally confirm |
| has a knowledge cutoff | Assume it's behind on the newest library versions |

### The Flip Side: It's Genuinely Brilliant

None of this means the AI is dumb. It will out-recall you on syntax, suggest approaches you didn't know, and write correct boilerplate instantly. The move isn't to distrust everything — it's to **calibrate**. Trust it like you'd trust a fast, well-read intern: lean on its speed and breadth, and put a check on anything that would hurt if it were wrong.

> **Key idea:** The AI optimizes for *plausible*, not *true*. Your job is to supply the truth it can't see (your code, your constraints) and to be the one source of *certainty* in the loop. Hold that model and the rest of vibe coding becomes common sense.

left.label: Human junior: 'I'm not sure, let me check the docs'

description.en: The AI cannot feel uncertain. Confident tone is not evidence of correctness.

explanation.en: This method does not exist in pandas
Close, but you have to know the difference
Verify against docs, not against the vibe

output: The AI optimizes for PLAUSIBLE, not TRUE.
You supply the truth it can't see.
You are the certainty in the loop.

instruction.en: An AI wrote this function to count unique words. It used a 'plausible' string method that doesn't exist (str has no .count_unique()). Fix it using real Python so it returns the number of distinct words. Expected output: 3

practice_challenges.starter_code:
def unique_word_count(text):
    words = text.split()
    # AI wrote: return words.count_unique()  <- not a real method!
    # Fix it using a real Python built-in:
    return 0  # replace this line

print(unique_word_count("red blue red green blue"))

en: A set keeps only distinct items.
len(set(words)) gives the number of unique words.
Distinct words here: red, blue, green -> 3.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert unique_word_count('red blue red green blue') == 3
assert unique_word_count('a a a') == 1
assert unique_word_count('') == 0

q.en: Why does a coding AI confidently suggest methods that don't exist?

en: It predicts plausible-looking text rather than looking up facts

q.en: How much should the AI's confident tone increase your trust in an unfamiliar API it suggests?

en: A lot — confidence means it's sure
Not at all — it sounds equally confident when right or wrong

q.en: The AI can't see your codebase. What's the best way to get code that fits it?

en: Hope it guesses your function names
Paste the real signatures, types, and constraints it must use
Ask it to remember last week's chat
Use a longer prompt with no specifics
